title: Descend Into a Volcano's Magma Chamber—No Lava Required
summary:
Have you ever dreamed of exploring the inside of a volcano? In Iceland, that dream is a reality. Thríhnúkagígur (pronounced three-NOO-kah-gee-gur), a dormant volcano near Reykjavík, is the only place on Earth where you can descend into an empty magma chamber—the underground cavern that once held molten rock before an eruption.

The volcano last erupted 4,500 years ago, and its magma chamber is completely empty. That's unusual. Normally, leftover magma slowly solidifies inside the chamber, filling the ground beneath the volcano. But at Thríhnúkagígur, the magma seems to have disappeared. Scientists think it may have been sucked back down into Earth's crust. "It's like somebody came and pulled the plug and all the magma ran down out of it," said Haraldur Sigurdsson, a volcanologist at the University of Rhode Island.

Since 2012, visitors have been allowed to enter the chamber via an open cable elevator built in 2010 for scientists. The descent is 700 feet (210 meters)—more than double the height of the Statue of Liberty with its pedestal. At the bottom, you can roam a 33,600-square-foot (3,120 square meters) cave floor, about the size of a soccer field.

The walls are decorated with vivid colors: bronze, indigo, yellow, and orange. These colors are attributed to microbes (tiny organisms) and sulfur-rich gases. Scientists aren't sure exactly which microbes live there. Occasionally, visitors see puffs of steam rising from corners of the chamber, but these are caused by water dripping onto lamps, not signs of an impending eruption. Evidence suggests Thríhnúkagígur has erupted only three times in the past 50,000 years, so it's quite safe.

The volcano's name means "Three Peaks Crater" in Icelandic, referring to three peaks at the surface. The youngest peak formed 4,500 years ago, the next 5,000 years ago, and the oldest 50,000 years ago, when Iceland was covered by a thick ice sheet. That oldest peak is made of hyaloclastite, a sharp, glassy volcanic rock formed when lava meets ice.

Would you be brave enough to take the elevator into a volcano's heart?
why_it_matters: This discovery shows that volcanoes can hold surprises that teach us about Earth's geology and how life can exist in extreme environments.

keywords.explanation: A volcano that has not erupted for a long time but could erupt again.
Changes from liquid to solid, like magma turning into rock.
The base that supports a statue.
A sharp, glassy volcanic rock formed when lava meets ice.
Containing a lot of sulfur, a chemical element that smells like rotten eggs.

questions.question: What is unusual about Thríhnúkagígur's magma chamber?
options: It is still full of lava.
It is the largest in the world.

questions.question: Who is Haraldur Sigurdsson?
options: A volcanologist at the University of Rhode Island.
The person who built the cable elevator.
questions.correct_answer: A volcanologist at the University of Rhode Island.

questions.question: How deep is the descent into the magma chamber?
What are the colors on the chamber walls caused by?
What is hyaloclastite?
options: A glassy rock formed when lava meets ice
A mineral found in volcanic ash
questions.correct_answer: A glassy rock formed when lava meets ice

background_read: Thríhnúkagígur is a dormant volcano in Iceland, a country known for its volcanic activity because it sits on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge and a hot spot.
Most magma chambers are filled with solidified rock after an eruption, but Thríhnúkagígur's chamber is empty—a rare geological puzzle.
Haraldur Sigurdsson, a volcanologist at the University of Rhode Island, has studied the volcano and compared the missing magma to 'somebody came and pulled the plug.'
The volcano's three peaks formed during different ice ages, and the oldest peak contains hyaloclastite, which forms when lava erupts under ice.
Article_Structure: LEAD: You can descend into a dormant volcano's empty magma chamber in Iceland.
└─ specific: 'It's like somebody came and pulled the plug' – volcanologist
KEY EVENT: Thríhnúkagígur volcano's magma chamber is empty and open to visitors since 2012.
├─ WHO: Visitors and scientists led by Haraldur Sigurdsson
├─ WHEN: Last erupted 4,500 years ago; opened 2012
└─ AMOUNT: 700 ft descent, 33,600 sq ft floor
EXPLANATION: Why the chamber is empty – magma may have drained back into crust
DETAILS: Colors from microbes and gases; safety from rare eruptions
CONTEXT: Three peaks formed over 50,000 years; hyaloclastite from ice age

perspectives.description: Tourists and scientists benefit from unique access to a natural wonder, and the site promotes volcanic education and adventure tourism.
Some worry that encouraging tourism inside a volcano could set a precedent for risky activities or disturb fragile geological features.
The chamber's emptiness poses a geological puzzle; studying it could improve understanding of magma movement and eruption prediction.
Exploring microbial life in the chamber may lead to discoveries about life in extreme environments, with implications for astrobiology.
